ocr.text: The analysis presented in the accompanying table investigates the efficacy of various glove sizes in achieving microbial
reduction, as measured by the mean log10-reduction across differing sample sizes. Specifically, the table highlights the mean
log10-reduction values, which serve as a critical metric in evaluating the performance of gloves in preventing contamination.
For instance, the results indicate a mean log10-reduction of 2.25 ± 1.20 across 62 subjects when using gloves of size 7,
suggesting a strong level of effectiveness. Additionally, the data demonstrates variability in reduction rates, with a notable mean
log10-reduction of 1.73 ± 0.86 observed in a smaller cohort of 11 subjects utilizing size 2 gloves. These findings underscore the
importance of glove size in infection control measures, particularly in settings where microbial exposure is a concern. The p-
value associated with these results further supports the statistical significance of the findings, providing a robust framework for
understanding the relationship between glove size and microbial reduction. Overall, this analysis contributes valuable insights
to the field of health and safety practices.
